pattern: Counting permutations where adjacent sums are perfect squares is a Hamiltonian-path count over a graph of compatible values, solved with backtracking that skips duplicate choices.
hints: Two values can be neighbours only when their sum is a perfect square; think of values as nodes with an edge when that holds.
You are counting distinct orderings, so sort and at each step skip a value equal to one you already tried from the same position.

Backtrack: place values one at a time, only extending to an unused value whose sum with the previous placed value is a perfect square, and divide out duplicate work by the skip rule rather than deduping whole permutations.

edge_inputs.description: all equal values count as one permutation
no adjacent pair forms a perfect square
two distinct values that sum to a square

stress.description: small array drawn from values likely to form square sums
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    pool = [0, 1, 2, 3, 7, 8, 9, 16, 17, 24, 25]
    n = max(1, min(size, 8))
    return [[rng.choice(pool) for _ in range(n)]]
stress.note: small n keeps the factorial backtracking fast
